statement: V1 is scoped to the discovery-manifest-compile-serve pipeline for 3 Node.js frameworks only. All 12 vision domains are deferred to V2+.
rationale: The vision is deliberately broad but V1 must ship a complete, testable vertical slice. Express, Fastify, and NestJS cover the majority of production Node.js API frameworks. Going deep on one ecosystem beats going shallow on many.

statement: MCP serve is describe-only in V1 — no live API proxying.
rationale: Live proxy requires auth forwarding, error normalization, and reliability handling. These are significant concerns that would delay V1. Describe-only MCP still proves the pipeline end-to-end and is useful for agent discovery.

statement: Capability grouping starts as 1:1 route-to-capability mapping. Intelligent grouping deferred.
rationale: Smart capability grouping (merging related endpoints into higher-level capabilities) is the core vision differentiator but requires heuristic tuning against real codebases. Starting 1:1 lets us ship and iterate based on real feedback.

statement: No plugin API in V1. Framework support is hardcoded for Express, Fastify, NestJS.
rationale: Premature abstraction of plugin interfaces risks building the wrong API. V1 hardcodes 3 frameworks; the plugin boundary emerges from the patterns we discover during implementation.

statement: TypeScript/JavaScript ecosystem only in V1. Python, Go, Java are V2+.
rationale: Single AST parser ecosystem (ts-morph or equivalent) lets us go deep on inference quality. Multi-language support multiplies the scanning surface without adding core product value.

statement: tusq review is non-interactive in V1 — prints summary to stdout only. Users approve capabilities by editing tusq.manifest.json directly. Interactive TUI deferred to V1.1.
rationale: Previous artifacts contradicted themselves: scope item 11 said 'interactive review/approval' but behavior section said 'prints to stdout'. V1 has no TUI — keeping it simple and consistent.

statement: tusq manifest requires an explicit prior tusq scan — no implicit scan fallback in V1.
rationale: Implicit scan is hidden complexity that makes the pipeline harder to reason about. Explicit steps are clearer for V1. Users run tusq scan then tusq manifest.

reasons: PM signoff is not approved. Found "Approved: NO" in .planning/PM_SIGNOFF.md; set it to "Approved: YES".

statement: Scan data persists to .tusq/scan.json as an internal file contract between tusq scan and tusq manifest.
rationale: The spec defined an implicit dependency between scan and manifest but never specified the intermediate file. Without this, an implementer would have to invent the storage location, risking divergence from spec intent.

statement: V1 includes automatic domain grouping — capabilities are grouped by resource name or controller (e.g., /users/* routes → 'users' domain). Cross-resource merging deferred to V1.1.
rationale: VISION.md mandates 'basic skill composition: domain grouping for generated tools' in V1. Pure 1:1 with no grouping contradicted the vision. Minimal domain assignment adds organizational value without complex heuristics.

statement: All planning docs now explicitly state V1 proves discovery + manifest + MCP exposure, not full executable capability delivery. tusq serve is describe-only.
rationale: Human feedback required honest product claims. V1 does not proxy live API calls — stating this clearly prevents overpromising and aligns expectations for V1.1 proxy work.

statement: Runtime learning is documented as a deferred core pillar of the tusq.dev vision, not a normal omitted feature. It is planned for V2.
rationale: Runtime instrumentation and production-signal-driven manifest refinement are foundational to the vision (the bridge from 'good enough' to 'continuously accurate'). Treating it as a minor omission misrepresented its importance.

statement: sensitivity_class field added to the canonical artifact (tusq.manifest.json and tusq-tools/*.json) with 5 levels: unknown, public, internal, confidential, restricted. V1 defaults to 'unknown' for all capabilities, matching the conservative-default pattern used for input/output schemas.
rationale: The vision (VISION.md line 57) explicitly requires 'side effects and sensitivity class' in the canonical artifact. Adding the field now with a safe default establishes the contract for downstream consumers without overclaiming inference accuracy. Humans can manually set sensitivity during manifest review.

statement: Side effects and sensitivity are modeled as two separate fields (side_effect_class + sensitivity_class) rather than a single combined classification. Side effect answers 'does this mutate state?' while sensitivity answers 'what kind of data does this touch?'
rationale: The vision mentions 'financial' and 'security-sensitive' alongside read/write/destructive. Conflating mutation type with data sensitivity in one field creates ambiguity (e.g., a POST /payments is both a 'write' and 'confidential'). Two fields let agent runtimes make orthogonal decisions: invocation gates (side effect) vs. audit/redaction rules (sensitivity).

statement: Challenged the previous turns' lack of formal side_effect_class documentation. rationale: The SYSTEM_SPEC listed side_effect_class values but did not explain the classification logic, the handler/path name matching for destructive detection, or why the system is intentionally conservative (preferring false positives over false negatives).

statement: Auth, side effects, and sensitivity are modeled as three separate governance dimensions rather than conflated into fewer fields. auth_hints answers 'what credentials are required?', side_effect_class answers 'does this mutate state?', and sensitivity_class answers 'what kind of data does this touch?'
rationale: Agent runtimes make three independent decisions: can I call this without confirmation (side effect), what audit/redaction rules apply (sensitivity), and do I have sufficient credentials (auth). Conflating these into fewer fields creates ambiguity and forces agent runtimes to over-approximate.

statement: V1 auth_hints remain identifier-based string arrays with no semantic parsing. V2 adds a structured auth_requirements object alongside the existing array for backward compatibility. Role extraction, scope mapping, tenant boundary detection, and least-privilege hints are all V2 goals.
rationale: Follows the same conservative-default pattern established for input_schema, output_schema, and sensitivity_class: ship the field, make the contract stable, defer inference accuracy to V2. Breaking changes to the artifact shape are worse than conservative V1 defaults.

statement: Version history uses three fields: manifest_version (integer counter at manifest root), previous_manifest_hash (SHA-256 hash chain at manifest root), and capability_digest (per-capability SHA-256 of content fields). V1 produces fields but ships no diff CLI or history file.
rationale: Follows the established conservative-default V1 pattern used for sensitivity_class, examples, constraints, and redaction. Fields establish the contract for V2 tooling without overclaiming V1 capabilities.

statement: Version history fields are manifest-only — they do not propagate to compiled tools (tusq-tools/*.json) or MCP responses (tools/list, tools/call). This matches the approval metadata pattern.
rationale: Version history tracks the evolution of the canonical artifact itself. Compiled tools are point-in-time snapshots of approved capabilities. MCP responses serve current state. Neither needs lineage information.

statement: capability_digest excludes approval/review metadata (approved, approved_by, approved_at, review_needed) and the digest itself. It includes all content fields (name, description, method, path, schemas, governance fields, provenance, confidence, domain).
rationale: The digest must answer 'did the capability itself change?' not 'did someone approve it?' Including approval state would cause false change signals on every human review action.

statement: Challenged prior turns for leaving framework support depth undocumented. V1 shipped three deep framework detectors but the planning artifacts never formally specified what 'deep' means per framework, why Node.js-only satisfies the vision requirement, or what the V2 expansion plan looks like.
rationale: VISION.md line 71 requires 'support the most common framework stacks deeply within the first release.' The implementation delivers depth but the planning artifacts didn't substantiate the claim. Without a formal spec, consumers of the planning docs cannot verify that V1 framework support actually meets the vision bar.

statement: V1 framework depth is defined as framework-specific extraction of 12 capabilities (method, path, handler, inline detection, middleware, auth hints, schema hints, domain, controller resolution, guard inheritance, deduplication, nearby code radius) rather than generic regex matching.
rationale: Each framework has fundamentally different routing conventions: Express uses app.verb() chains, Fastify supports both inline and route-object patterns, NestJS uses decorators with controller-level inheritance. A single generic pattern would miss framework-specific signals (NestJS guard inheritance, Fastify route blocks, Express middleware chains).

statement: Node.js-only V1 scope satisfies the vision's 'most common framework stacks' requirement because Express, Fastify, and NestJS cover the majority of Node.js backend APIs, and the vision explicitly prioritizes depth over breadth (line 114).
rationale: VISION line 114: 'Support fewer stacks, deeper. Node plus Python plus one or two serious backend frameworks, done correctly, beats thin support for twelve.' Python is mentioned as a V2 target alongside the Node.js stacks. V1 delivers the Node.js portion deeply; V2 adds Python via the plugin interface.

statement: V2 framework expansion will use the plugin interface (extractRoutes standard interface) rather than core code changes, enabling community framework adapters.
rationale: VISION line 130: 'Framework detectors... all live behind plugin interfaces. The core stays small. The ecosystem grows around it.' This is the architectural boundary that makes width sustainable.

statement: Fastify inlineWithOptionsPattern captures handler expression as 'async function listOrders(' (boundary at listOrders() closing paren), but extractIdentifiers correctly isolates 'listOrders' after filtering blocked tokens; extraction is correct despite the regex boundary quirk.

statement: Challenged ALL prior turns (DEC-143 through DEC-223) for addressing governance metadata dimensions while leaving the core extraction output — the fields an LLM actually reads to understand a tool — functionally useless on first pass. VISION.md lines 72-73 explicitly require usable first-pass manifests and treat manual authoring as engine failure.
rationale: Prior turns added sensitivity_class, auth_hints, examples, constraints, redaction, versioning, and framework depth specifications. All are necessary governance metadata. But a manifest where every capability description says 'GET /users capability in users domain', every input_schema is { type: object, additionalProperties: true }, and domain inference produces 'api' instead of 'users' requires complete manual rewrite before an LLM can use it. The governance decorations are meaningless if the base extraction is unusable.

statement: Path parameters (:id, {id}) extracted from route paths into input_schema.properties as required string fields. Uses regex on information already in the route path — no AST parsing needed.
rationale: An LLM receiving { type: 'object', additionalProperties: true } for a tool at /users/:id has no idea it needs to provide an 'id' argument. Extracting the parameter name from the path and adding it as a required property is the single highest-impact improvement for first-pass usability.

statement: Domain inference skips well-known API prefixes (api, v1-v5, rest, graphql, internal, public, external) before selecting the first meaningful path segment.
rationale: The current inferDomain() takes the literal first segment, so /api/v1/users produces domain 'api'. This cascades into wrong capability names and meaningless grouping. Skipping prefixes is a 10-line change that fixes domain accuracy for the majority of real-world route paths.

statement: Capability descriptions use a rich template (verb + noun + qualifier + side_effect + auth_context + handler) instead of the static 'METHOD /path capability in domain domain' string.
rationale: The description field is what an LLM reads to decide whether a tool is relevant. 'Retrieve user by id — read-only, requires requireAuth (handler: getUser)' provides semantic context. 'GET /api/v1/users/:id capability in api domain' provides none. All template fields are already computed before describeCapability() runs.

statement: Confidence scoring applies a -0.10 penalty when no schema hint is detected, pushing schema-less routes with named handlers and auth below the review_needed threshold of 0.8.
rationale: A route scoring 0.86 with additionalProperties: true and no real schema misleads reviewers into thinking the engine understood the route well. The penalty makes the manifest honest: routes where the engine could not extract parameter structure are flagged for review.

statement: Opt-in is carried by a new governance artifact .tusq/execution-policy.json (schema_version, mode: describe-only | dry-run, optional allowed_capabilities, reviewer, approved_at) and a single new CLI flag tusq serve --policy <path>. Without --policy, tusq serve behavior is byte-for-byte identical to V1 describe-only. The approval gate is preserved as an invariant: only approved: true capabilities appear in tools/list, and allowed_capabilities is a strict subset filter on top of approval, never a replacement.
rationale: A governance artifact is reviewable the same way a manifest is reviewable; this keeps operator adoption explicit rather than implicit and gives the launch phase something concrete to document as a reviewable file.

statement: The dry-run response from tools/call under mode: dry-run ships with an explicit executed: false marker, a policy echo block (mode, reviewer, approved_at), and a dry_run_plan object with fields method, path, path_params, query, body, headers, auth_context, side_effect_class, sensitivity_class, redaction, plan_hash, and evaluated_at. plan_hash is a SHA-256 of a canonical JSON serialization of {method, path, path_params, query, body, headers} and deliberately excludes evaluated_at, policy echoes, and executed so identical validated inputs produce identical hashes.
rationale: Challenge 19 — plan_hash is the first replay primitive on the serve path. Shipping it now prevents a breaking shape change later when V2 adds confirm/replay/plan-diff tooling.

statement: Argument validation depth for V1.1 is intentionally narrow: required field enforcement, primitive type checks (string/number/integer/boolean), and additionalProperties: false rejection for schemas that declare it. No format, enum, oneOf, or regex validation in V1.1. Validation errors are surfaced as JSON-RPC -32602 with data.validation_errors: [{path, reason}].
rationale: Narrow validation covers the argument failure modes an LLM actually produces (missing required, wrong type, made-up property) without committing to an external JSON Schema validator dependency or a reimplementation. Deeper validation is deferred to V2.

statement: M21 scope is exactly one new CLI command: `tusq policy init`. It scaffolds a valid `.tusq/execution-policy.json` file that passes the M20 `loadAndValidatePolicy()` validator byte-for-byte. No interactive wizard, no manifest read, no auto-suggested allowed_capabilities, no multi-environment profiles, no policy migration, no signed-policy work, and absolutely no live API execution under any code path. Rejected three alternatives explicitly: (A) interactive wizard — coupled to TTY state and hostile to CI; (B) a `--init` flag on `tusq serve` — conflates server startup with governance-artifact generation; (C) accepting the V1.2 scope as 'sufficient without a dedicated command' — fails the charter's reduce-manual-setup goal.
rationale: The charter is narrow by design: one rung of the VISION operator-ergonomics ladder. A small, non-interactive generator under a new `policy` noun leaves room for V2 siblings (`policy suggest`, `policy migrate`, `policy verify`) without later renaming and matches the naming pattern of existing `tusq approve`, `tusq diff`, `tusq docs`, and `tusq compile` commands.

statement: Default `mode` for a generated policy is `"describe-only"`; `dry-run` requires an explicit `--mode dry-run` flag. This is codified as a new SYSTEM_SPEC.md Constraint 8 (M21 safe-default invariant) and as a separate Key Risk row in ROADMAP.md.
rationale: If the generator defaulted to dry-run, an operator who ran `tusq policy init` followed by `tusq serve --policy <file>` would silently flip their MCP server from describe-only to dry-run plan emission. That is the exact quiet-governance-change mistake the M20 launch-messaging work (DEC-487, DEC-488) had to correct in the messaging layer; reintroducing it in the tooling layer is unacceptable.

statement: Generated policy files MUST pass `loadAndValidatePolicy()` byte-for-byte. Smoke coverage will perform a round-trip (generate → validate) to catch any drift between the M21 generator and the M20 validator. This is codified as a new SYSTEM_SPEC.md Constraint 7 (M21 local-only invariant) and enumerated in the Docs and tests required before implementation section.
rationale: The validator and generator are two independent pieces of logic that describe the same file shape. Without a round-trip test, the two can drift silently — a future contributor could add a field to the validator without updating the generator (or vice versa), and operators would hit cryptic failures at `tusq serve --policy` time. The round-trip collapses both into one enforced contract.

statement: M22 is exactly one new CLI subcommand, `tusq policy verify`, under the existing `policy` noun.
rationale: A dedicated verifier gives CI and pre-commit workflows a safe policy-file gate without starting the MCP server or conflating serve startup with validation.

statement: `tusq policy verify` must reuse `loadAndValidatePolicy()` byte-for-byte with `tusq serve --policy`.
rationale: The verifier is only trustworthy if a policy that passes verify starts serve, and a policy that fails verify fails serve startup with the same message.

statement: Manifest-aware strict validation is explicitly out of scope for V1.3.
rationale: Reading `tusq.manifest.json` would expand the verifier beyond a policy-file validator and violate the local-only/no-manifest-read boundary for this increment.

statement: cmdPolicyVerify calls loadAndValidatePolicy() un-modified, with no standalone re-implementation of validation logic. Error messages emitted to stderr (or --json stdout) are the CliError.message values thrown by the shared validator.

statement: On --json, the JSON result object goes to stdout in both success and failure cases; exit code is the authoritative success signal. On non-json, success goes to stdout and failure to stderr.

statement: M23 scope is exactly one new flag (--strict) and one supporting flag (--manifest <path>, consulted only under --strict) on the existing tusq policy verify subcommand. Under --strict, the verifier additionally reads tusq.manifest.json and cross-references every name in allowed_capabilities against the manifest's approval-gated set (existence + approved:true + NOT review_needed:true). Default behavior (no --strict) MUST remain byte-for-byte identical to M22. Rejected three alternatives explicitly: (A) new tusq policy audit subcommand — fragments the governance surface and the parity contract; (B) making --strict the default when a manifest exists — silently breaks every M22 caller and couples verify to filesystem state; (C) adding a freshness or execution-reachability probe — violates the local-only invariant.
rationale: The charter asks for least-privilege governance via opt-in strict verification while preserving local/offline/no-live-execution defaults. A flag on the existing subcommand (rather than a new subcommand) keeps the governance surface small, honors the M22-ledgered intent from Challenge 25, and makes the scope exactly additive — every M22 contract survives. Opt-in (not default) preserves backward compatibility for CI jobs validating pre-manifest scaffolds.

statement: Strict-mode failure messages are NOT part of the M22 validator-parity contract. tusq serve --policy does NOT enforce strict checks and never will under V1.4 — serve continues to silently drop unlisted/unapproved capabilities from tools/list as specified in M20. Strict verify is a CI/review-layer gate, not a serve-startup gate. Codified as two new SYSTEM_SPEC.md Constraints: 11 (M23 opt-in-strict invariant — default path never opens the manifest, --strict is never inferred, --manifest without --strict exits 1 before any file read) and 12 (M23 least-privilege-validation invariant — strict PASS is a policy/manifest alignment statement at verify time only, MUST NOT be framed as runtime safety, execution pre-flight, manifest freshness, or reachability probe).
rationale: The single biggest foot-gun in manifest-aware verification is narrative drift: a PASS being misread as runtime safety. Codifying the forbidden framings as constraints in SYSTEM_SPEC rather than in launch-artifact prose makes them enforceable across dev/qa/launch roles and inherited by every downstream doc. Keeping serve unchanged preserves the M20 approval-gate invariant exactly.

statement: Identified and fixed a concrete drift between two PM-owned gate artifacts: .planning/ROADMAP.md line 220's M23 smoke checklist enumerated 8 items (a–h) while .planning/SYSTEM_SPEC.md line 2155's docs/tests-before-implementation list enumerated 12 items. Three items were in SYSTEM_SPEC but absent from ROADMAP: (1) --strict exit 1 on malformed manifest JSON, (2) --manifest without --strict exits 1 with '--manifest requires --strict' before any file read (the Constraint 11 ordering guard), and (3) M22 parity under --strict (every existing M22 failure fixture still produces byte-identical messages with and without --strict). Extended the ROADMAP checklist with three new items (i), (j), (k) that cover these three smoke cases; did NOT shrink SYSTEM_SPEC.md (the spec is the normative contract and inherits from Constraints 9/11, not vice versa).
rationale: ROADMAP.md is the checkable list dev treats as the implementation acceptance surface; SYSTEM_SPEC.md is the normative contract QA uses to re-verify. If they disagree, dev ships against ROADMAP, QA holds dev to SYSTEM_SPEC, and the two artifacts produce conflicting verdicts on the same PR. Silent omission of the --manifest ordering guard is especially risky because it is encoded in Constraint 11 — if dev never sees it as a ROADMAP checkbox, the guard can be omitted from the first implementation and leak into QA as a gap. Bringing the checklist up to the spec is a zero-scope change that closes the disagreement window before dev starts.

statement: Rejected two tempting alternatives during the smoke-list alignment: (A) shrink SYSTEM_SPEC to match the shorter ROADMAP — rejected because the spec is the normative contract; dropping malformed-JSON, --manifest ordering-guard, or parity tests would weaken the governance contract for the sake of a shorter ROADMAP row; (B) leave the drift and let QA add the missing smoke items as REQ-075+ — rejected because QA adds acceptance criteria, not smoke-test requirements; if ROADMAP does not ask dev to write a smoke test, dev will not write one, and QA cannot assert on a test that does not exist.
rationale: Making the decision explicit in PM_SIGNOFF Challenge 29 prevents a future reader (or a regenerated planning turn) from silently flipping the direction of alignment. The ROADMAP-inherits-from-SYSTEM_SPEC direction is a governance invariant of this project; it should be ledgered once and defended.

statement: Extended cmdPolicyVerify in src/cli.js with --strict (boolean) and --manifest <path> (value) flags. Constraint 11 guard exits 1 with '--manifest requires --strict' before any file read when --manifest is set without --strict. M22 validator runs first on every code path; strict checks only execute after M22 validation passes, preserving the M22 parity invariant byte-for-byte.

statement: Built a Map<name, capability> from manifest.capabilities for O(1) lookup, then iterated allowed_capabilities in declaration order to produce strict_errors. This guarantees deterministic ordering (matches allowed_capabilities array order) with no Set-based deduplication that could reorder entries non-deterministically.
rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC specifies strict_errors are collected in deterministic order (the order names appear in allowed_capabilities). A Map lookup + linear scan of allowed_capabilities is the simplest correct implementation; Set-based deduplication would risk non-deterministic ordering on V8 insertion-order changes.

statement: null/unset allowed_capabilities passes the strict check trivially without reading any capability from the manifest. An empty array also passes trivially. Only a non-empty array triggers the per-name cross-reference loop.
rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC strict check rules: null/unset means 'all approved capabilities in the manifest are allowed' which is by definition manifest-aligned; empty array authorizes zero capabilities so no misalignment is possible. Both cases must pass with no manifest capability read.

statement: M24 scope is exactly one new static-extraction path: literal Fastify route-options `schema: { body: { properties, required } }` → manifest `input_schema.properties`, `input_schema.required`, `input_schema.additionalProperties: false`, `input_schema.source: 'fastify_schema_body'`. No new CLI command, no new flag, no new subcommand. Fastify-only; other framework extractors (Express, NestJS) produce byte-identical manifests pre/post-M24. Rejected three alternatives: (A) ship Fastify + Express-validator + NestJS DTO inference in one increment — requires AST parsing / TS compiler API / new dependencies, violates zero-new-dependency invariant and ~0.5-day scope; (B) ship a thin express-validator chain extractor alongside Fastify — either supports 30% of patterns (creates a long tail of unsupported cases) or requires a chain-expression parser (new dependency); (C) defer M24 entirely — violates the charter and leaves a concrete VISION.md line-72 gap unfilled when the fix is in the deterministic zone.
rationale: Fastify `schema.body` is the only Node-ecosystem validator shape that is both deterministic (literal JSON Schema by convention) and regex-extractable with balanced-brace matching. It fits a bounded ~0.5-day increment that produces real code, tests, docs, QA evidence, and launch artifacts without expanding the dependency footprint or attack surface.

statement: M24 extraction is active by default on source-literal pattern match; no new CLI flag, no environment variable gate. A route that does not declare a literal Fastify `schema.body` block gets M15 fall-back behavior byte-for-byte. The M13 `capability_digest` re-computation and M13 approval-gate flow handle the migration path: an M24-driven `input_schema` change flips the digest, `tusq diff` surfaces it, and `tools/list` continues to filter by `approved: true` until a reviewer explicitly re-approves the updated capability. Rejected two alternatives: (A) a `tusq scan --extract-fastify-schemas` opt-in flag — creates two competing manifest shapes for the same repo, invites operators to forget the flag, and would require a matching flag for every future validator extractor; (B) an environment-variable gate (`TUSQ_FASTIFY_SCHEMA_EXTRACTION=1`) — worse than (A) because environment state is invisible in the manifest itself.
rationale: M15 correctly made path-parameter extraction the default because the source is unambiguous; M24 applies the same principle to Fastify `schema.body`. The approval-gate invariant (M20 Constraint preservation) already covers the drift-and-review workflow.

statement: Codified two new SYSTEM_SPEC constraints. Constraint 13 (M24 static-literal-extraction invariant): regex + balanced-brace matching only; no import of fastify/ajv/@sinclair/typebox; no eval/new Function/ts-node; any parse ambiguity drops the entire `schema_fields` record and falls back to M15 byte-for-byte; partial extraction is forbidden; non-Fastify fixtures produce byte-identical manifests pre/post-M24. Constraint 14 (M24 source-literal-framing invariant): the extracted shape is 'what the source declares,' NOT 'what the runtime enforces'; docs/README/CLI-help/launch artifacts MUST NOT describe the shape as 'validator-backed,' 'runtime-validated,' 'ajv-validated,' 'Fastify-enforced,' or any phrase that implies runtime enforcement; defensible framing is 'the declared body schema as it appears literally in source.'
rationale: Constraint 13 protects the local-only / zero-new-dependency invariants that have held since M14. Constraint 14 heads off the narrative risk that M23 Constraint 12 addressed at a different surface (alignment-statement vs runtime-safety), applied to the extraction layer.

statement: Added extractBalancedBlock() and extractFastifySchemaBody() as pure helper functions in src/cli.js before extractFastifyRoutes(). extractBalancedBlock skips string literals while counting open/close chars. extractFastifySchemaBody implements the 8-step SYSTEM_SPEC algorithm: schema:{, body:{, properties:{, top-level field iteration in source order, required:[ parsing. Any failed step returns null and the caller falls back to M15 byte-for-byte.
rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC Constraint 13 requires regex+balanced-brace matching only; no framework import, no eval. A separate helper function keeps the extraction logic testable and isolated from the route-building logic. The fail-fast null return on any parse failure enforces the partial-extraction-is-forbidden invariant.

statement: Extended the fastify.route({...}) pattern (routePattern) and the 3-arg inline pattern (inlineWithOptionsPattern) in extractFastifyRoutes to set schema_fields: extractFastifySchemaBody(block) on each route object. The simple inline pattern (inlinePattern, no options block) does not receive schema_fields since there is no options block to extract from.
rationale: Only the two patterns that capture an options block can contain a schema: key. The simple inline form fastify.verb(path, handler) has no options object so schema extraction is not applicable.

statement: Added score += 0.04 in scoreConfidence() when route.schema_fields is truthy, after the existing schema_hint branch. This gives a route with both schema_hint and schema_fields a total schema signal of +0.18 (capped at 0.95). Routes without schema_fields retain byte-identical confidence scores.
rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC specifies +0.04 additive confidence boost for extracted schema fields as a stronger signal than the existing +0.14 schema-hint boolean. The cap at 0.95 is preserved.

statement: Updated buildInputSchema() to handle route.schema_fields: when schema_fields.body.properties exists, merge declared fields into properties (skipping path-param name collisions via a Set), merge required entries (skipping already-listed path params), and return input_schema with additionalProperties: false and source: 'fastify_schema_body' at the top level. The M15 fall-back path (generic body placeholder, additionalProperties: true) is untouched.
rationale: Path parameters are authoritative for URL shape (SYSTEM_SPEC §M24 Constraint 13 and pipeline table). Placing source on input_schema itself (not on individual properties) matches the SYSTEM_SPEC pipeline diagram: input_schema { properties, required, source: 'fastify_schema_body', additionalProperties: false }.

statement: Extended the Fastify fixture from 2 to 5 routes: POST /items (literal schema.body with name/price/active), PUT /items/:id (path-param collision: body declares id:number but path param id:string wins), GET /catalog (schema: sharedSchema variable reference, exercises fall-back). Existing GET /orders and POST /orders routes are unchanged.
rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC docs-and-tests requirements explicitly enumerate these three fixture cases. Using fastify.route({...}) form for the new complex routes guarantees the routePattern regex correctly captures the full nested object block.

statement: Added fastify-schema-body-extraction-determinism eval scenario to governed-cli-scenarios.json and runFastifySchemaExtractionScenario() to eval-regression.mjs.
